examples.title: Adding a computed property to an interface you don't own
examples.description: The new `extension` block lets you declare a real property on `IEnumerable<T>` — no helper class, no method call syntax required at the call site.

examples.title: Adding a static factory member to an existing type
examples.description: Extension members aren't limited to instance members — you can attach static properties and methods so callers use `DateTime.Yesterday` as if it shipped in the BCL.

examples.title: Building a fluent extension block for a domain type
examples.description: Group multiple extension properties and methods in a single `extension` declaration to create a cohesive, discoverable surface on a type you don't control.

relatedFeatures.reason: Start with Extension methods to understand the C# 3.0 pattern that extension members supersede and extend.
Explore Static abstract interface members to see how static members on interfaces complement extension members in library design.
Continue to LINQ for the most influential example of the extension-method pattern that extension members now elevate.
